ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term exists for Chicken Majestic; mapped to broader 'chicken food product' class as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Chicken Majestic; A0CKN (chicken meat, cooked) is the closest defensible parent match for this deep-fried and sautéed chicken preparation. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for this Hyderabadi composite dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept found for Chicken Majestic or a sufficiently close Hyderabadi chicken preparation.

allergens: gluten (possible cross-contamination if wheat flour added)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Chicken Majestic is approximately 150 g, typically presented as a restaurant appetizer portion of 6–8 crispy chicken strips tossed in yogurt-spice tempering.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Chicken Majestic exists in ICMR-NIN tables. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for deep-fried chicken preparations and cooked chicken with spices, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) entries for fried chicken and cornstarch-battered poultry to arrive at the composite per-100g profile. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy fried chicken data (NDB 05062). Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat set to -1 as detailed fatty acid breakdown for this specific preparation is not available in either ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central for the exact composite dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — peer-reviewed analogous estimate for cornstarch-battered fried chicken
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chicken Majestic. GI of 45 is estimated by analogy with cornstarch-battered fried chicken preparations documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021). The high protein content (16.5 g/100g) and fat content (13.5 g/100g) from the chicken and frying oil moderate the glycemic response of the cornstarch coating (9.2 g carbohydrate/100g). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (45 × 13.8) / 100 ≈ 6.2, where carbohydrate per 150g serving = 9.2 × 1.5 = 13.8 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (420 mg/100g; ~630 mg per 150g serving) from seasoning, marinade, and deep-frying process
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Chicken Majestic due to its high sodium content of approximately 630 mg per standard serving, which represents over 27% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg; if consumed, portion size should be restricted to 75–100g and paired with low-sodium accompaniments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from cornstarch batter (9.2 g/100g) offset by high protein (16.5 g/100g) and fat (13.5 g/100g), yielding a low estimated GI of 45 and glycemic load of ~6.2 per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chicken Majestic has a low estimated glycemic index (~45) and a moderate glycemic load (~6.2 per 150g serving), making it a cautiously acceptable occasional choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, the deep-fried preparation adds significant fat calories and the sodium content warrants monitoring, so portion control to 100–120g and infrequent consumption are advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation contributes elevated total fat (13.5 g/100g) including saturated fat (~3.2 g/100g), combined with high sodium (420 mg/100g), both of which are established cardiovascular risk factors
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat Chicken Majestic as an occasional indulgence only, given its deep-fried fat content and high sodium level; when consumed, a serving of no more than 100g is recommended, and it should not be paired with other high-sodium or high-fat foods in the same meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger) in the marinade may offer modest benefit, but deep-frying introduces pro-inflammatory oxidized fats
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Chicken Majestic contains turmeric and ginger with documented anti-inflammatory properties, but the deep-fried preparation may introduce pro-inflammatory lipid oxidation products; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should consume it infrequently and monitor personal inflammatory responses.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Majestic is a popular Hyderabadi appetizer originating from Telangana, India, characterized by thin, elongated strips of chicken that are first marinated in a mixture of buttermilk and ginger-garlic paste, then lightly coated in a cornstarch-based batter and deep-fried until crispy. The fried strips are subsequently tossed in a hot tempering of garlic, green chilies, and curry leaves, then finished with a tangy yogurt-based sauce seasoned with turmeric and cumin, giving the dish its distinctive yellow-orange hue and creamy-tangy flavor profile. It is closely related to but distinct from Chicken 65, differing in the use of thin strips rather than chunks, the buttermilk marinade, and the characteristic yogurt tempering finish. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides approximately 220 kcal, 16.5g protein, 13.5g fat, and 9.2g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, moderate-fat snack. The sodium content is relatively high at 420 mg/100g, which is a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The estimated glycemic index is low (~45), moderated by the high protein and fat content relative to the cornstarch carbohydrate load. Clinically, it is best consumed in controlled portions by individuals with metabolic conditions. It is commonly served as a restaurant starter, party appetizer, or high-protein snack at lunch or dinner.
common_user_queries: How is Chicken Majestic different from Chicken 65?
Is Chicken Majestic good for diabetics?
Can I eat Chicken Majestic on a diet?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Chicken Majestic exists in any approved primary source (ICMR-NIN, USDA FoodData Central). Nutritional values are adapted from ICMR-NIN deep-fried chicken preparations and USDA SR Legacy fried chicken data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for cornstarch-battered fried chicken. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' for deep-fried chicken preparations and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 05062, fried chicken) as no exact Chicken Majestic entry exists in any approved source. Saturated fat (3.2 g/100g) estimated from USDA SR Legacy fried chicken data. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, cholesterol, and moisture data for this specific Hyderabadi composite preparation are not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. 3. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on cornstarch-battered fried chicken in the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2021); no direct GI measurement for Chicken Majestic has been published. 4. Portions array in input contained three identical duplicate entries (150g); deduplicated to a single canonical serving of 150g. 5. This is an Indian / Hyderabadi dish; multilingual naming rules for Singaporean dishes do not apply. Hindi and Telugu names are provided as the dish originates from Telangana (Hyderabad). Chinese, Malay, and Tamil local names are not applicable and set to null. 6. Halal flag set to true as the dish is traditionally prepared with halal chicken in Hyderabad; consumers should verify halal certification at point of purchase. 7. FoodEx2 match type corrected from 'adapted' to 'closest_parent' to conform to the allowed values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'] as required by G5 ontology honesty gate.
